Manually remove all members from IAS group OR delete the group entirely

8. Removing the Restriction (Post Go-Live)

Important: Removing the IPS condition does NOT automatically remove users from the IAS group. IPS only adds or updates group membership — it never deletes it. Manual removal of IAS group members is always required when lifting a restriction.

Step

Action

Details

1

Remove Group Rule from IPS Source Transformation

Delete the entire condition mapping block from the source transformation. Save the transformation.

2

Run IPS Jobs in Sequence

READ on IAS source → RESYNC on SF source. IPS will no longer re-add users to the group since the condition has been removed.

3

Manually Remove Group Members in IAS

IAS → Users & Authorizations → User Groups → Restricted_Users_Group → select all members → Remove. Alternatively, delete the group entirely if no longer needed.

4

Delete IAS Rules from All Applications

For every SF application in IAS, remove the Conditional Authentication Priority 1 rule AND the Risk Based Authentication DENY rule.

5

Verify SSO Works for Previously Restricted Users

Test 3–5 previously restricted users — they should now be redirected to Azure AD and login successfully via normal SSO.

9. Key Lessons Learned

#

Lesson

Why It Matters

1

Always run READ on IAS source BEFORE Resync

Populates IPS UUID cache needed for group resolution

2

Always use RESYNC job — not READ job

READ job only caches data internally and never writes to IAS target

3

Never filter sf.user.filter to a single user

Causes mass deletion of all other users in IAS on next Resync

4

No resolveEntityIds for manually created IAS groups

Silently drops group assignment with no error shown in logs

5

Conditional Auth Priority 1 rule is mandatory

Without it Azure AD authenticates users before DENY rule can fire

6

DENY rule belongs in Risk Based Auth — not Conditional Auth

Conditional Auth only routes users to IdPs — it cannot deny access

7

SF userId can be numeric — always verify exact value

The userId and Username fields are completely different in SF

8

custom02 must be listed in sf.user.attributes

Field never returned by SF OData API if not explicitly listed

9

IAS group name is case sensitive

Must match the IPS transformation constant value exactly

10

Removing IPS condition does not remove IAS group membership

Manual removal from IAS group is always required after lifting restriction

11

Groups mapping must be in USER section of target transformation

Group section may be set to ignore:true — placing mapping there has no effect

12

Use custom02 + exclusion list for large user sets

Shorter condition, auto-applies to new hires, much easier long-term maintenance

Summary: Restricting access in SAP SuccessFactors via IAS and IPS requires a three-layer approach — IPS transformation tags users with a restriction group, IAS Conditional Authentication routes them to IAS native login at Priority 1, and IAS Risk Based Authentication DENY rule blocks access. Users remain active in IAS throughout. The most critical insight: Conditional Authentication and Risk Based Authentication must work together — one routes the user, the other denies them. Neither works alone for this use case. When lifting the restriction, always manually remove members from the IAS group as removing the IPS condition alone does not trigger group membership deletion.

This blog documents a real implementation with real challenges encountered and resolved. Every troubleshooting point above was an actual issue from the project. I hope this helps others working on similar access restriction requirements with SAP SuccessFactors, IAS, and IPS. Feel free to leave questions in the comments!

entry.title: Enabling IAS for SAP SuccessFactors RCM and RMK(Internal Career Site)
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog post, I will walk through the process of enabling Identity Authentication Service (IAS) for SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting Management (RCM) and Recruiting Marketing (RMK) for an Internal Career Site. While the configuration steps are straightforward, ensuring that each component is correctly aligned is critical, as small inconsistencies can lead to issues such as login loops, failed redirects, or incomplete authentication.

This guide covers the required prerequisites, configuration steps, and troubleshooting scenarios based on practical observations to help you implement a secure and seamless authentication experience.

 

Prerequisites

Before enabling IAS for the Internal Career Site, ensure the following prerequisites are completed.

1. Identity Authentication Service (IAS)

IAS must be enabled for your SAP SuccessFactors instance. IAS acts as the Identity Provider and handles user authentication. Without IAS, SAML-based login for the Internal Career Site cannot be configured.

2. Career Site Builder Setup

Career Site Builder (CSB) must be configured and integrated with Recruiting Management (RCM). This integration enables seamless navigation and authentication between the career site and recruiting modules.

3. Recruiter Single Sign-On (SSO)

Recruiter SSO should be enabled to provide a consistent authentication experience across Recruiting Management and Recruiting Marketing.

If Recruiter SSO is not maintained, recruiters will not experience true single sign-on. Even after successfully logging in, they will be prompted to enter their username and password again when navigating between interconnected modules such as RCM and RMK. This breaks the seamless user experience and can create confusion, especially during testing, as it may appear that IAS is not functioning correctly.

4. Career Site and Recruiting Management Integration

Career Site–RCM redirects must be enabled.

Navigation:
Provisioning → Configure Recruiting Marketing Datacenter URL

Maintain the Recruiting Marketing datacenter URL that corresponds to your tenant region. 

If the datacenter URL is incorrect, users may experience failed redirects when accessing the internal career site. In practice, this can appear as the page not loading correctly or users being redirected to an invalid or blank page. In some cases, authentication seems to initiate but does not complete, which can be misleading since the issue is related to incorrect endpoint configuration rather than IAS itself.

5. Career Site Builder Integration Settings

Navigation:
Admin Center → Manage Career Site Builder → Site Configuration → Site Integration

Verify the following configurations in Career Site Builder Integration Settings:

  • Company ID
    Copy the Company ID from Provisioning. In training instances, ensure it is maintained in ALL CAPS.

  • Deployment Data Center
    Select the correct data center where your SAP SuccessFactors HCM instance is hosted.

  • Single Sign-On Security Key
    Maintain the security key (20–28 characters). This key acts as a bridge between RCM and RMK and is required for secure communication.

  • Recruiter Sync (Single Sign-On)
    Enable Recruiter Sync to allow synchronization of recruiter information and provide access to Career Site Builder and Recruiting Advanced Analytics.

In practice, when any of these configurations are incorrect or inconsistent, the integration between RCM and RMK does not fail immediately but leads to issues during usage. Recruiter Sync may not function properly, recruiters may be unable to access Career Site Builder, and job or candidate data may not synchronize as expected. These issues can be misleading, as the initial configuration appears complete, but the breakdown in connectivity between systems becomes visible only when recruiters start working with the system.

6. Mobile Apply and Internal Career Site

Ensure both Mobile Apply and Internal Career Site are enabled.

Navigation:
Admin Center → Manage Recruiting Sites

These settings allow employees to access and apply for internal jobs. If the Internal Career Site is not enabled, users will not be able to access internal opportunities even if IAS is configured successfully.


Step 1: Configure SAP SuccessFactors (RCM) for IAS

The first step is to establish trust between SAP SuccessFactors and Identity Authentication Service (IAS). This allows SuccessFactors to redirect users to IAS for authentication and accept the SAML assertion returned after a successful login.

Navigation

Go to:

Admin Center → Manage Service Provider Configuration for Identity Authentication Service

Enable SAML Integration

Under the Import IdP SAML Metadata section:

  1. Turn on SAML Integration
  2. Maintain the IAS Tenant Name

Note:
The tenant name is derived from the IAS URL.

Example:

https://a122u123.accounts.cloud.sap

Tenant Name: a122u123

An issue we encountered during this step was maintaining an incorrect tenant name. Although the configuration appeared to be correct, it resulted in an endless login loop where users were redirected to IAS, entered their credentials, and were sent back to the login page again instead of being authenticated. This made it difficult to identify the root cause initially, as the issue appeared to be related to authentication rather than a simple tenant mismatch.

Export Metadata

After completing the configuration:

  1. Click Save
  2. Click Export Metadata

Expected outcome: The metadata file is successfully generated and available for download. This file will be imported into IAS when creating the RCM application.

Step 2: Configure IAS Application for Recruiting Management (RCM)

Next, create an application in IAS to establish the trust relationship with your SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting Management instance. This application will use the metadata exported in the previous step to enable SAML-based authentication.

Navigation

Go to:

Applications & Resources → Applications

Create New Application

Click Create and maintain the following details as shown in the configuration screen:

  • Display Name: RCM_Tenant_name
  • Home URL: Leave blank (or maintain if required by your landscape)
  • Type: SAP SuccessFactors solution
  • Parent Application: None
  • Organization ID: global
  • Protocol Type: SAML 2.0

After maintaining the above details, click Create.

Configure SAML 2.0 Settings

  1. Open the newly created application
  2. Navigate to SAML 2.0 Configuration
  3. Upload the metadata file that was exported from SuccessFactors (RCM system)

Configure Certificate Settings

  1. Go to the Certificates section
  2. Maintain:
    • Signing Algorithm: SHA-256

SHA-256 is the recommended signing algorithm and helps ensure secure SAML communication between IAS and SuccessFactors.

Maintain Subject Name Identifier

This is one of the most important settings in the configuration, as it determines how IAS identifies users when sending the SAML assertion to SuccessFactors.

Maintain the following values:

  • Source: Identity Directory
  • Attribute: Login Name

The attribute specifies which user field from IAS will be passed to SuccessFactors as the user's identifier. In our case, we used Login Name as the attribute, but this can vary depending on your organization’s configuration. It is important to ensure that the selected attribute aligns with the user identifier maintained in SuccessFactors, otherwise users may authenticate in IAS but not be able to access the system.

Summary

At this stage, IAS has been enabled for SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting Management (RCM), and the required SAML configuration has been completed. The system is now prepared for the next phase of the Internal Career Site authentication setup.

Step 3: Configure Career Site Builder (RMK) for IAS

After enabling IAS for Recruiting Management (RCM), the next step is to establish trust between Recruiting Marketing (RMK) / Career Site Builder (CSB) and IAS. This allows employees accessing the Internal Career Site to be redirected to IAS for authentication instead of using the default login mechanism.

Navigation

Log in to your SuccessFactors instance and go to:

Admin Center → Manage Career Site Builder

Then navigate to:

Settings → IDP Configuration

Maintain IAS Tenant Details

In the Import IDP SAML Metadata section:

  1. Enter the IAS Tenant Name
  2. Click Save

The tenant name identifies which IAS tenant RMK should use for authentication. If the tenant name is incorrect, users may be redirected to the wrong authentication endpoint or encounter SAML-related errors when attempting to access the Internal Career Site.

Note:
Make sure you save the configuration before proceeding to metadata generation.

Generate and Export Metadata

After saving:

  1. Click Generate
  2. Click Export Metadata

Expected outcome: RMK metadata is generated successfully and can be imported into the RMK application in IAS.

The exported metadata file contains the RMK Service Provider information that IAS requires to establish trust. Think of this metadata as the configuration blueprint that allows IAS and RMK to recognize and trust each other during the authentication process.

Step 4: Create IAS Application for RMK

With the RMK metadata available, the next step is to create a dedicated application in IAS. Although Recruiting Management (RCM) and Recruiting Marketing (RMK) are closely connected, each application requires its own trust configuration in IAS.

Log in to the SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) Admin Console.

Navigation

Go to:

Applications & Resources → Applications

Create New Application

Click Create and maintain the following details (as shown in your configuration screen):

  • Display Name: RMK_Tenant_name
  • Home URL: Leave blank (or maintain if required)
  • Type: SAP SuccessFactors solution
  • Parent Application: None
  • Organization ID: global
  • Protocol Type: SAML 2.0

Click Create.

Configure SAML 2.0 Settings

  1. Open the newly created RMK application
  2. Navigate to SAML 2.0 Configuration
  3. Upload the metadata file exported from Career Site Builder

This step completes the trust configuration from the IAS side and allows IAS to recognize authentication requests originating from RMK.

Configure Signing Settings

  1. Go to Certificates
  2. Verify:
    • Signing Algorithm: SHA-256

This ensures that SAML assertions exchanged between IAS and RMK are signed using a secure and supported algorithm.

Maintain Subject Name Identifier

Ensure consistency with the RCM configuration:

  • Subject Name Identifier
    • Source: Identity Directory
    • Attribute: loginName

Important Notes

  • Always save changes before generating metadata in Career Site Builder
  • Separate applications must be created in IAS for:
    • RCM
    • RMK
  • Ensure the same Subject Name Identifier configuration is maintained across all applications
  • Ensure that the IAS application is created as a standalone application and not as a child application

Troubleshooting

Issue 1: Internal candidate unable to apply and repeatedly redirected to sign-in page

One issue I encountered during testing was an endless redirect loop when attempting to access the Internal Career Site. Authentication appeared to start correctly, but users were continuously returned to the sign-in page.

After investigation, the root cause was a tenant domain mismatch. The system was configured with the legacy domain format:

<tenant_name>.accounts.ondemand.com

However, the instance had already been migrated to the Common Super Domain (CSD) framework and was using:

<tenant_name>.accounts.cloud.sap

  • Ensure that this updated tenant name is maintained consistently in:

    • RCM (Manage Service Provider Configuration)
    • Career Site Builder → IDP Configuration

  • Save the changes and retry the access.

Issue 2: User successfully authenticates in IAS but cannot access the Internal Career Site

If users can log in through IAS but are not successfully signed in to SuccessFactors, verify the Subject Name Identifier configuration.

The attribute selected in IAS must correspond to the user identifier maintained in SuccessFactors. If the values do not match, IAS can authenticate the user successfully, but SuccessFactors will be unable to identify the corresponding account.

Review the Subject Name Identifier configuration in both the RCM and RMK IAS applications and confirm that the selected attribute aligns with your organization's user mapping strategy.

Conclusion

Enabling IAS for SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting Management (RCM) and Recruiting Marketing (RMK) requires configuration across multiple components, including SuccessFactors, Career Site Builder, and IAS. While the setup itself is straightforward, attention to details such as tenant configuration, metadata exchange, and user identifier mapping is critical for a successful implementation.

By following the steps outlined in this guide, you can establish the trust relationships required for SAML-based authentication and provide a seamless sign-on experience for internal career site users. If issues arise, validating tenant settings and authentication mappings early in the troubleshooting process can help resolve most common configuration problems.

entry.title: Principal Propagation from BTP Web App to S/4HANA via SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

During a recent customer PoC, I worked on a scenario where a BTP-hosted web application needed to call an S/4HANA on-premise API — not as a generic service account, but as the actual logged-in user. 
This is where Principal Propagation becomes essential. Preserving user identity end-to-end isn't just a technical nicety — it matters for auditability, compliance, and access control. When S/4HANA receives a call as the real user, every action is traceable: security audit logs show who did what, authorizations are enforced per user, and there's no shared service account masking individual activity.

In this post, I share the configuration and lessons learned that made it work—from a BTP web application authenticated via SAP Cloud Identity Services, through SAP Integration Suite and Cloud Connector, all the way to S/4HANA, with user identity preserved throughout the entire chain.

Thanks to @cj_lo  for preparing Cloud Connector with S/HANA On-premise demo system, testing, and troubleshoot together.

Scenario

A simple web application is deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. When the user clicks a button, it triggers an iFlow on SAP Integration Suite that fetches data from S/4HANA. The S/4HANA system must receive the call as the authenticated user — not as a technical service account. 
While direct access to S/4HANA via a BTP Destination and SAP Cloud Connector is possible, many organizations choose to introduce a middleware layer to support centralized governance, security, monitoring, and error handling. Ultimately, the target architecture is customer-specific and should balance integration requirements, operational considerations, governance needs, and cost.

Browser (logged-in user)
  → BTP Web App (via Approuter + Cloud Identity Services)
    → BTP Destination Service
      → SAP Integration Suite iFlow
        → Cloud Connector
          → S/4HANA (on-premise, as the user)

Prerequisites & Scope

This article assumes the following components are already installed and configured:

  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry environment
  • SAP Integration Suite
  • SAP Cloud Connector connected to the SAP BTP subaccount
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS) as the Identity Provider
  • SAP S/4HANA system accessible through Cloud Connector
  • User identities mapped between IAS and SAP S/4HANA

This article focuses on the principal propagation scenario from a SAP BTP application through SAP Integration Suite to an on-premise SAP S/4HANA system. Topics such as SAP Cloud Connector installation, SAP BTP onboarding and trust configuration, access control setup, certificate management, and SAP S/4HANA user provisioning are outside the scope of this article. If I were to cover all of them here, I would end up writing a book instead of a mini blog 😉

Relevant SAP documentation is listed in the References section at the end of this article.

Solution Overview

The solution relies on three key components working together:

ComponentRole
ApprouterHandles IAS login, holds the user's JWT token
BTP Destination (OAuth2JWTBearer)Exchanges the user's IAS JWT for an Integration Suite-scoped token using the PI Runtime service key
iFlowReceives the user-scoped token, calls S/4HANA via Cloud Connector with Principal Propagation

The critical insight is using OAuth2JWTBearer as the destination authentication type. This is the only type that correctly takes the user's IAS JWT and exchanges it at the PI Runtime token endpoint — preserving the user identity all the way to SAP Integration Suite.

Configuration

The BTP app consists of the following files — each is covered in the steps below:

your-app/
├── manifest.yml
├── package.json
├── xs-app.json
├── xs-security-ias.json
└── dist/
    └── index.html

Provision BTP Cloud Foundry Services

Before deploying the app, create the required service instances in your Cloud Foundry (CF) space.
Below using CF CLI, if you need a crash course using it, please check out this tutorial here.

Target your CF API endpoint and log in (in this example my region is jp10, yours could be different):

cf api https://api.cf.jp10.hana.ondemand.com
cf login

Create a Destination service instance (used by the Approuter to resolve BTP destinations), you can replace iflow-pp-destination-svc with service name of your choice:

cf create-service destination lite iflow-pp-destination-svc

Create an IAS (Identity) service instance using the xs-security-ias.json security descriptor, you can replace iflow-ias-svc with service name of your choice:

cf create-service identity application iflow-pp-ias-svc -c xs-security-ias.json

Where xs-security-ias.json contains:

{
  "xsappname": "iflow-pp-ias",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "credential-type": "X509_GENERATED",
  "oauth2-configuration": {
    "redirect-uris": [
      "https://*.cfapps.jp10.hana.ondemand.com/login/callback?authType=ias"
    ],
    "post-logout-redirect-uris": [
      "https://*.cfapps.jp10.hana.ondemand.com/*/logout.html"
    ]
  }
}

credential-type: X509_GENERATED uses a certificate-based credential instead of a client secret — recommended for production.

Both service instances are bound to the app via manifest.yml:

applications:
  - name: poc-iflow-pp-fe
    memory: 512M
    buildpacks:
      - nodejs_buildpack
    services:
      - iflow-pp-ias-svc
      - iflow-pp-destination-svc

Ensure the User's Email Matches S/4HANA Identity

The Cloud Connector Subject Pattern (configured under Cloud To On-Premise → Principal Propagation) determines how the X.509 certificate CN is built. The default pattern is:

CN=${name}

${name} is sourced from the user_name claim in the PI Runtime token. During the OAuth2JWTBearer exchange, PI Runtime populates user_name from the email claim of the incoming IAS JWT — not from sub or any other claim.

So the identity that reaches Cloud Connector and ultimately S/4HANA is always the user's email address.
This behaviour is important because many troubleshooting efforts focus on the IAS sub claim, while principal propagation in this flow ultimately depends on the user's email address being propagated and resolvable by the backend system.

Important: Make sure the user's email address in IAS matches a user identity that S/4HANA can resolve — either via direct email-to-username mapping on the S/4 side, or as the logon name if S/4 uses email as the logon name format.
Depending on your identity governance requirements, the Cloud Connector Subject Pattern can use mapping logic beyond a basic CN. On the S/4HANA side, certificate-to-user mapping (CERTRULE) adds a second layer of control, allowing a certificate CN to be mapped to a different logon name. For complex scenarios, refer to the Cloud Connector Subject Pattern Syntax and S/4HANA Certificate-Based User Mapping documentation to design patterns that fit your enterprise architecture.

To verify what identity Cloud Connector is propagating, check CC Admin UI → Logging and Tracing — the outbound request will show the CN value used in the short-lived certificate.

Here's how my configuration looks like in Cloud Connector:

 Here's how rule-based certificate mapping looks like in S/4HANA - /ncertrule

Create Process Integration Runtime Service Instance

You probably has setup this one in order to trigger any published iFlow; or if you have one but has not included below grant type, then you can create a new one in BTP Cockpit → Service Marketplace → Process Integration Runtime:

  • Plan: integration-flow
  • Grant Types to enable:
    • client_credentials (for testing)
    • jwt bearer (the one that we need)
    • refresh_token
    • saml2 bearer
  • Create a Service Key and note down clientid, clientsecret, and tokenurl

Why PI Runtime and not IAS credentials? In my landscape setup, SAP Integration Suite only trusts tokens issued by its own OAuth authorization server (via the PI Runtime instance - current the SAP Integration Suite is authenticated via SAP Default idP). IAS tokens forwarded directly are not trusted by SAP Integration Suite unless full IAS-to-Integration Suite trust federation is set up. The PI Runtime service key is the standard supported path.

Configure BTP Destination

In BTP Cockpit → Connectivity → Destinations → New Destination:

FieldValue
Name<unique destination name, i.e. m25_iflow>
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://<your-cpi-host>/http/<your-iflow-endpoint>
Proxy TypeInternet
AuthenticationOAuth2JWTBearer
Client ID<clientid from PI Runtime service key>
Client Secret<clientsecret from PI Runtime service key>
Token Service URL<tokenurl from PI Runtime service key>

No additional properties required.

Common pitfalls:

  • Do not use OAuth2UserTokenExchange — it uses refresh_token grant internally which PI Runtime does not support
  • Do not use IAS credentials here — SAP Integration Suite will reject them
  • The destination name must exactly match what is referenced in xs-app.json (watch for underscore vs hyphen)

BTP App — Approuter Setup

The frontend app is a plain HTML file served by the SAP Approuter (@sap/approuter). No custom middleware needed.

package.json:

{
  "name": "ar-iflow-pp-fe",
  "dependencies": {
    "@sap/approuter": "^22.0.3"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node node_modules/@sap/approuter/approuter.js"
  }
}

xs-app.json — routes inbound requests to the BTP destination or local static files:
Also remember to double-check the Destination name here in case you're using different name.

{
  "welcomeFile": "/index.html",
  "authenticationMethod": "route",
  "logout": { "logoutEndpoint": "/logout" },
  "routes": [
    {
      "source": "^/user-api(.*)",
      "target": "$1",
      "service": "sap-approuter-userapi"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/m25_iflow(.*)$",
      "destination": "m25_iflow",
      "csrfProtection": false,
      "target": "$1",
      "authenticationType": "ias"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/(.*)$",
      "localDir": "dist",
      "authenticationType": "ias",
      "cacheControl": "no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate"
    }
  ]
}

The /user-api route uses the built-in Approuter user info service — no custom code required to read the logged-in user's details.

dist/index.html — the entire frontend in one file:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <title>Principal Propagation PoC</title>
  <style>
    body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 40px auto; padding: 0 16px; }
    button { padding: 8px 20px; margin-top: 8px; cursor: pointer; }
    pre { background: #f4f4f4; padding: 12px; white-space: pre-wrap; word-break: break-all; margin-top: 8px; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>

<h2>Principal Propagation PoC</h2>

<h3>Logged-in User</h3>
  <pre id="userInfo">Loading...</pre>

<h3>Trigger iFlow</h3>
  <button onclick="callIflow()">Call iFlow (GET /m25_iflow)</button>
  <pre id="iflowResponse">Response will appear here</pre>

<script>
    // Fetch logged-in user info from built-in Approuter user API
    fetch("/user-api/currentUser")
      .then(r => r.json())
      .then(u => document.getElementById("userInfo").textContent = JSON.stringify(u, null, 2))
      .catch(e => document.getElementById("userInfo").textContent = "Error: " + e.message);

// Trigger iFlow via BTP Destination (OAuth2JWTBearer)
    function callIflow() {
      call("/m25_iflow");
    }

function call(url) {
      document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = "Calling " + url + "...";
      fetch(url, {
        headers: { "X-Requested-With": "XMLHttpRequest" }
      })
        .then(async r => {
          const text = await r.text();
          try { return "HTTP " + r.status + "\n\n" + JSON.stringify(JSON.parse(text), null, 2); } catch (_) { return "HTTP " + r.status + "\n\n" + text; }
        })
        .then(body => document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = body)
        .catch(e => document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = "Error: " + e.message);
    }
  </script>

</body>
</html>

Deploy:

cf push

Assign ESBMessaging.send Role to Users (IAS Group Mapping)

In BTP Cockpit → Security → Role Collections → Create (e.g. CPIMessagingSend) :

Roles tab — add:

Application IdentifierRole TemplateRole
it-rt-<your-instance>ESBMessagingESBMessaging.send

User Groups tab — add:

Identity ProviderName
<your-ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com — Business UsersIntegration_Senders

Make sure your users are members of the Integration_Senders group in IAS, and select Business Users (not Application Users) as the user type — logged-in app users are business users in IAS.

iFlow configuration summary: The iFlow uses an HTTPS Sender Adapter with User Role authorization (ESBMessaging.send) and an OData/HTTP Receiver Adapter configured for On-Premise proxy type with Principal Propagation authentication. See the Integration Flow section below for the full configuration.

Building Integration Flow

This section assumes you are familiar with creating integration flows in Cloud Integration of SAP Integration Suite.

The integration flow in this demo scenario is intentionally minimal — its purpose is to receive the inbound call from the BTP web app, forward it to S/4HANA via Cloud Connector with Principal Propagation, and return the response.

The iFlow contains the following steps:

  1. HTTPS Sender Adapter — receives the inbound HTTP request from the BTP Approuter
  2. Request-Reply step — forwards the call to the S/4HANA OData service
  3. OData V2 Receiver Adapter — calls S/4HANA on-premise via Cloud Connector

HTTPS Sender Adapter configuration:

FieldValue
Address/your-iflow-endpoint
AuthorizationUser Role
User RoleESBMessaging.send

OData V2 Receiver Adapter — Connection tab:

FieldValue
Addresshttp://<virtual-host>:<port>/sap/opu/odata/sap/<service-name>
Proxy TypeOn-Premise
AuthenticationPrincipal Propagation
Location ID<your Cloud Connector location ID>
Custom Query Parametersap-client=<client no.>

OData V2 Receiver Adapter — Processing tab:

FieldValue
OperationQuery (GET)
Resource Path<EntitySetName>
Query Options$top=10 (or as needed)
Custom Query Options<SAP Client Number>

The sap-client parameter must go in Custom Query Parameters (Connection tab). The Resource Path must have no leading slash — e.g. `A_GLAccountInChartOfAccounts`, not `/A_GLAccountInChartOfAccounts`.

Testing the Flow

With the web app deployed, open it in the browser. You will see the logged-in user's identity displayed (via the /user-api/currentUser endpoint exposed by the Approuter). Click Call via iFlow (GET /m25_iflow) button to trigger the integration flow.

A successful end-to-end flow will show the same user email across the BTP app, the Integration Suite message log, and the S/4HANA audit log. The sections below describe how to verify each layer.

Cloud Connector — Trace

If the call reaches the Cloud Connector but something goes wrong on the S/4HANA side, enable tracing in the Cloud Connector Admin UI:

  1. Go to Cloud Connector Admin UI → Logs and Trace → Trace (Debug level)
  2. Trigger the iFlow again from the web app
  3. View the trace (scc_core.trc) and search for the CN value — this confirms which user identity Cloud Connector placed in the short-lived X.509 certificate sent to S/4HANA

This is especially useful when the call appears to succeed from the Integration Suite side but S/4HANA returns a 401 or an authorization error — it lets you confirm the CN matches a user S/4HANA can resolve.

S/4HANA — ICF Trace (`/nsectrace`)

For the deepest level of verification, enable ICF tracing on the S/4HANA side to see exactly what the system receives at the HTTP layer:

  1. In S/4HANA, run transaction /nsectrace
  2. Navigate to ICF Service / Path and enable the trace for the relevant ICF service path (e.g. `/sap/opu/odata/sap/<service-name>`) and click Activate Trace
  3. Trigger the iFlow again from the web app
  4. Back in /nsectrace, click Deactivate Trace, and choose Show Trace — the trace output will show the inbound request headers including the ssl-client-subject field, which contains the CN from the Cloud Connector certificate

The CN value here is what S/4HANA evaluates for user mapping. If it does not match a known logon name, the call will fail with a 401 even if everything upstream is correct.

Key Takeaways

Principal Propagation is more than a technical configuration—it enables auditability, compliance, and least-privilege access control by ensuring S/4HANA receives requests as the actual business user rather than a shared service account.

The implementation approach depends on customer requirements and the overall solution architecture. Whether user identity is propagated through direct connectivity or a middleware layer, the objective remains the same: maintain end-to-end user context while balancing integration needs, governance, operational considerations, and cost.

One of the key lessons learned is that principal propagation depends on several components being configured consistently across BTP, Integration Suite, Cloud Connector, and S/4HANA. Even small trust or authorization gaps can prevent user identity from being propagated successfully, making end-to-end validation essential.

References

The following SAP documentation was useful while implementing and troubleshooting this scenario.

SAP BTP - Cloud Foundry Environment

  • Administration and Operations in the Cloud Foundry

SAP Cloud Connector and S/4HANA (ABAP)

  • Cloud Connector Documentation
  • Installation Guide
  • Managing Subaccounts
  • Principal Propagation Configuration
  • Configure Access Control (HTTP)
  • Configure Subject Patterns
  • Subject Pattern Rules API
  • S/4HANA / ABAP – Rule-Based Certificate Mapping (CERTRULE)

SAP Integration Suite

  • SAP Integration Suite Documentation
  • Cloud Integration Documentation

SAP Cloud Identity Services

  • Identity Authentication (IAS) Documentation
  • Destination Service Documentation
  • OAuth2JWTBearer Authentication

entry.title: Q2 2026: Quarterly Release Highlights for SAP BTP Security and Identity & Access Management
entry.content:
The world of security never stands still, and neither do we. Here's a quick look at the latest SAP BTP Security and Identity & Access Management updates from this quarter, with the features and improvements worth adding to your radar.  

For a complete overview of feature deliveries for SAP Cloud Identity Services, check out our list of all new feature announcements in the SAP Cloud Identity Services Release Notes on the SAP Help Portal.

SAP Cloud Identity Services: Technical users

SAP Cloud Identity Services now supports technical users—a new user type designed for non-human identities that enable secure, automated system-to-system communication and integrations. Technical users are managed separately from regular users through the new Technical Users tile under Users & Authorizations in the administration console, making it easier to create, organize, and maintain service identities. For step-by-step instructions,  see Managing Technical Users.

SAP Cloud Identity Services: Block and delete users

Keeping your Identity Directory clean has just become easier. You can configure SAP Cloud Identity Services to automatically delete users who were created but never signed in to an application, helping reduce inactive accounts and simplify user lifecycle management. We’ve extended this functionality by adding a grace period. For configuration details, check Block or Delete Users Due to Inactivity.

SAP Cloud Identity Services: Automatic creation of Identity Provisioning tenants on SAP Cloud Identity for Neo tenants

Starting June 10, 2026, SAP automatically creates a new Identity Provisioning tenant on the SAP Cloud Identity infrastructure for every existing Identity Provisioning tenant running on the Neo environment that is linked to a shared IAM tenant with Identity Authentication.

This automatic provisioning gives you access to the latest Identity Provisioning capabilities and configuration options available on the SAP Cloud Identity infrastructure—features that are not supported on Neo. The newly created tenant is used for integrations related to bundled SAP cloud solutions, helping prepare your landscape for future enhancements. For migration details, see Migrate Identity Provisioning Bundle Tenant.

SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI: Now available in China

Organizations in China can now subscribe to the SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI and run the service on infrastructure located within China. This availability helps customers meet local deployment requirements while modernizing authentication for SAP GUI. They will be able to simplify integration by connecting more easily with enterprise identity providers, and strengthen security with enhanced support for multifactor authentication (MFA).

You can see the supported data centers here. For more information on the SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI, check the SAP Help Portal.

Application Vulnerability Report for SAP BTP

In December 2025, SAP introduced the Application Vulnerability Report (beta) for SAP BTP, providing an API-driven way to identify open-source vulnerabilities in your Cloud Foundry applications.

The service has now been enhanced with its first graphical user interface, available directly in the SAP BTP Cockpit. The new interface features an intuitive findings dashboard with a severity breakdown, along with a detailed findings view that provides actionable remediation guidance to help you address identified vulnerabilities.

The Application Vulnerability Report is currently available as a beta service in the EU10 region. While subscriptions are not yet available in other SAP BTP landscapes, the service's scanning scope has been expanded significantly. It now scans applications deployed across 42 Cloud Foundry landscapes worldwide, including regions in the United States, Asia Pacific, Japan, Brazil, and additional European landscapes beyond the original EU10 region.

Stay in the loop

Join the SAP BTP Security and SAP Cloud Identity Services communities to get updates, share feedback, and connect with others!
